## Heart disease: Monitoring
- Symptoms and treatment
- Prevention and risks
- For health professionals
- How Canada monitors heart disease
- Rates       of heart disease

## How Canada monitors heart disease
The Public Health Agency of Canada uses  provincial and territorial health data from the Canadian Chronic Disease  Surveillance System. This system helps the federal government better understand  chronic conditions by estimating:
- those who are newly diagnosed with a chronic       condition (incidence)
- those who are living with a diagnosed chronic       condition (prevalence)
- the use of health services
Data  is used to plan and evaluate policies and programs.

## Rates of heart disease
Heart disease is the second leading cause  of death in Canada, causing over 55,000 deaths each year.
According to 2023 to 2024 data from the  Canadian Chronic Disease Surveillance System, 8.2% of adults in Canada were  living with diagnosed ischemic heart disease. It can lead to narrow arteries  due to plaque buildup (a mix of fats, calcium and cholesterol). This is the  most common type of heart disease, affecting about:
- 10% of males aged 20 years and older
- 7% of females aged 20 years and older
Males have higher hospitalization and  death rates related to heart diseases than females in all age groups, but the  difference lessens with age. However, because females live longer than males,  there were a similar number of heart disease-related deaths overall.

### High blood pressure (hypertension)
About 25% of adults in Canada aged 20  years and older are living with diagnosed hypertension. Males and females were  equally affected. Hypertension is when pressure is too high in your blood  vessels.
- High  blood pressure (hypertension)
About 3% of adults in Canada aged 20  years and older had survived a stroke. Males and females were equally affected,  but more females than males who survived a stroke died each year since they  tend to live longer.
Over half of people in Canada who  reported having a stroke rated their health as only fair or poor. In  particular:
- 23% self-reported diagnosed mood disorders
- about half self-reported mobility limitations
Clinical depression is very common among  people with heart diseases and conditions.
There are many symptoms associated with  depression, but the 2 main ones are a sad mood or loss of interest or pleasure in  activities. Symptoms must be present most days and for at least 2 weeks.
Depression can be treated. Watching for  early signs is important so that you seek help as soon as possible.  If someone close to you is showing signs, talk to them about getting help.
- Improving  your mental health
- Mental  health support: Get help
- How  to cope with stressful life events
- Depression  (Centre for Addiction and Mental Health)
